points.text: Split the sequential external calls into parallel async tasks to reduce overall latency.
Run EXPLAIN ANALYZE on the SQL to locate slow joins and missing indexes.
Add indexes on foreign‑key columns used in JOIN and WHERE clauses.
Select only required columns instead of SELECT * to shrink row size.
Consider materialized views or denormalization for frequently joined data.
Apply pagination or LIMIT on large result sets to avoid full table scans.
Set the endpoint timeout to the 95th‑percentile response time plus a safety buffer (e.g., +20%).
Parallelize independent service calls using async/await or a thread pool to cut sequential latency.
Wrap each external call with a circuit breaker to fail fast and prevent cascading timeouts.
Profile the SQL query; add indexes on join keys and consider covering indexes to speed up joins.
Refactor the join into smaller sub‑queries or CTEs that filter early, reducing row‑set size.
Set a total endpoint timeout (e.g., 2 s) and allocate per‑step budgets (e.g., 500 ms for each service, 500 ms for DB).
If result set is large, add pagination or limit clauses to avoid long‑running scans.
Move sequential external calls to parallel async tasks to reduce overall latency.
Profile the query with EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS) to identify slow joins and missing indexes.
Add btree indexes on foreign-key columns used in joins and on filter predicates.
Replace unnecessary many‑to‑many joins with sub‑queries or CTEs that pre‑aggregate data.
Consider materialized view for static reference data to avoid repeated joins.
Push down predicates to earliest possible join to improve selectivity.
Use LIMIT/OFFSET or keyset pagination to avoid returning huge result sets.
Set PostgreSQL statement_timeout (e.g., 2000ms) and enforce an app‑level timeout budget (3‑5s total).
Cache frequent reporting results in Redis or similar to bypass DB for repeated calls.
Ensure connection pool size matches concurrency to avoid queuing delays.
Batch sequential DB calls into a single query or use parallel execution to reduce round‑trip latency.
Create covering indexes on columns used in joins, filters, and order by to avoid full table scans.
Select only needed columns; replace SELECT * with explicit column list to reduce I/O.
Run EXPLAIN ANALYZE to identify costly joins and rewrite them using appropriate join order or CTEs.
Materialize intermediate results in temporary tables or indexed CTEs when joining many large tables.
Implement pagination or LIMIT clauses for large result sets to keep query time bounded.
Set endpoint timeout to 2–3× the 95th‑percentile query latency measured in production.
Offload heavy reporting to read replicas or a dedicated reporting database to avoid contention on OLTP nodes.
Split the endpoint into independent sub‑calls and run them concurrently (e.g., async/await or thread pool).
Reduce the SQL join depth: fetch only needed columns, replace heavy joins with pre‑aggregated tables or CTEs.
Add appropriate indexes on join/filter columns; consider covering indexes to avoid lookups.
Use pagination or LIMIT to bound result size; materialize frequent aggregates in a cache or summary table.
Allocate timeout budget: e.g., total 2 s → DB 1 s, network 0.3 s, processing 0.5 s, reserve 0.2 s headroom for retries.
